title: By Another Name
caption: When a child is taken, the world knows the word for it. Unless that word is adoption.
artworkAlt: A weathered house and ladder beneath a vast sky, with a curtain blowing from an open window
body:
Hello.

My name could be that of many. History remembers some of them. Others, it has quietly misplaced. But every one of them begins the same way: with a child and a disappearance.

My name is Charles Augustus Lindbergh Jr. I was twenty months old when someone climbed a ladder into my nursery in East Amwell, New Jersey, on the night of March 1st, 1932 and carried me out of my crib.

The world erupted.

More than a hundred thousand people joined the search. Police, soldiers, reporters, volunteers. Newspapers roared with headlines. Radios carried updates into living rooms across America.

The journalist H. L. Mencken called it “the biggest story since the Resurrection.” America called it the crime of the century. Because a child had been taken.

And when a child is taken, the world knows the word for it.
fullBody:
Hello.

My name could be that of many. History remembers some of them. Others, it has quietly misplaced. But every one of them begins the same way: with a child and a disappearance.

My name is Charles Augustus Lindbergh Jr. I was twenty months old when someone climbed a ladder into my nursery in East Amwell, New Jersey, on the night of March 1st, 1932 and carried me out of my crib.

The world erupted.

More than a hundred thousand people joined the search. Police, soldiers, reporters, volunteers. Newspapers roared with headlines. Radios carried updates into living rooms across America.

The journalist H. L. Mencken called it “the biggest story since the Resurrection.” America called it the crime of the century. Because a child had been taken.

And when a child is taken, the world knows the word for it.

---

My name is Jaycee Dugard. I was eleven years old when a car stopped beside me as I walked to my school bus stop in Lake Tahoe, California. Two adults stepped out. Seconds later I was gone.

My face travelled the country on missing posters. My name appeared on television broadcasts. People searched forests, roadsides and empty fields hoping to be the one who found me.

For eighteen years, I lived inside a hidden backyard prison. But the world never stopped calling my name.

Because when a child disappears, people recognise the crime.

---

My name is Paul Fronczak. I was one day old. A woman dressed as a nurse lifted me from my crib at Chicago’s Michael Reese Hospital in 1964 and walked out the door with me. The search for me became the largest manhunt in Chicago’s history.

Police mobilised. Volunteers combed the city. Newspapers ran my photograph again and again. A newborn had been stolen.

The world understood immediately what had happened.

---

My name is Jonathan Lyon. I was twelve months old when I was taken. But there were no sirens. No search parties. No posters stapled to telephone poles. No reporters asking where I had gone. No manhunt. No headlines.

Because my story had been given another word, adoption.

A word that quiets the alarms. A word that smooths the violence of separation into something polite and celebratory. Where other stolen children inspire outrage, adopted children are expected to inspire gratitude. The same rupture told in a different language.

From the outside, it looks like kindness. A child saved. A family formed. A life improved. People smile when they say the word. They imagine rescue. They imagine love.

They imagine a story that begins badly and ends well. But the child at the centre of that story still experiences the same first moment. The same vanishing. The same unanswerable question.

Where did my mother go?

> Anna Freud once wrote:
> “The horrors of war pale beside the loss of a mother.”

It is a sentence that unsettles people. War feels louder. Larger. More catastrophic. But the psyche does not measure loss in explosions. It measures it in rupture.

In the sudden disappearance of the person whose voice, smell, warmth, and heartbeat formed the entire architecture of the child’s world.

For some children, the world names that rupture honestly, Abduction. The word triggers sirens, search dogs and headlines. It mobilises a society. It declares: something terrible has happened here.

For others, the world chooses a softer story. A Hallmark version. One where loss becomes rescue. Where severance becomes salvation. Where the stolen child grows up smiling in a framed photograph. And the word used for that story is Adoption.

The world knows the names of children like Charles Lindbergh Jr. The world remembers Jaycee Dugard. The world searched desperately for Paul Fronczak.

Their stories live in history because society recognised what had been done to them. A child had been taken.

But there are millions of other children whose disappearances were never called crimes. Their stories begin with the same loss. The same severing. The same erased beginning. Yet the world tells them they were lucky.

Lucky to be chosen. Lucky to be loved. Lucky to have been given a better life. And so they grow up inside a strange contradiction. Carrying a wound the world insists is a gift.

Clawing through the rubble of their own erased origins, fingernails packed with the dust of a demolished beginning. Trying to explain a grief that others cannot see. Because the language used to describe what happened to them refuses to acknowledge that anything was lost at all. But every story in this piece begins the same way.

> A child.
> A disappearance.
> A mother gone.

Only the name of the crime changes.
beforeReading: This essay places three publicly documented abduction cases beside my own experience of being adopted at twelve months old. It isn’t an argument that every adoption is legally equivalent to abduction, nor does it deny the love that may follow. It asks what becomes invisible when the child’s first separation is named only through the intentions of adults.
originTitle: The alarm changes when the language changes.
origin: I kept returning to the asymmetry in our public language. When a child disappears, alarm is immediate. When an infant or child loses the first person they know as home through adoption, the story often begins with rescue, gratitude, and family formation. I wanted to place those two vocabularies beside one another and see what the contrast revealed. The historical cases give the essay its public scale. My own arrival at twelve months gives it its personal stake.
meaningTitle: A name can permit grief, or make it disappear.
meaning: This piece clarified that naming isn’t decoration. The word chosen for an event decides whose pain is visible, what grief is permitted, and which questions are considered impolite. For me, calling the beginning relinquishment rather than accepting adoption as the whole story is part of reclaiming authorship. It doesn’t cancel the life or love that came afterwards. It refuses to let them erase what came first.
recognitionTitle: The argument was recognised from inside the experience.
recognitionIntro: A response from an adoptee reader when this essay was first published on Substack.
seoTitle: By Another Name | Adoption and the Language of Loss
seoDescription: Jonathan Lyon examines how the language of adoption can quiet public recognition of separation, loss, and the child at the centre of the story.
